title: Mixed Graph: Average hours worked per week and stress-related illness across eight occupations
description: The charts show the average number of hours worked per week and the proportions of people suffering from stress-related illness across eight different occupations. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
guide_html:
HƯỚNG DẪN VIẾT BÀI

Working Hours & Stress-Related Illness by Occupation
1. Dạng bài

Mixed charts: Bar chart + Pie chart. Nội dung: working hours + stress levels by occupation.

2. Cách viết Overview
  • Giờ làm việc: chênh lệch lớn giữa các nghề — Business men làm nhiều nhất, Lecturers ít nhất
  • Stress: Lawyers cao nhất, Programmers thấp nhất
  • Không phải nghề làm nhiều giờ nhất sẽ có stress cao nhất
3. Cách chia Body
BODY 1 – Bar chart (Working hours) – theo ranking
  • Cao nhất: business men (~70h)
  • Tiếp theo: movie producers, doctors
  • Trung bình: writers, programmers, lawyers
  • Thấp nhất: chefs, lecturers (~25h)
BODY 2 – Pie chart (Stress levels) – theo tỷ lệ
  • Cao nhất: lawyers (25%)
  • Tiếp theo: movie producers (18%), doctors (15%)
  • Trung bình: business men, chefs
  • Thấp nhất: programmers (5%)
  • → stress không hoàn toàn phụ thuộc vào số giờ làm
4. Gợi ý từ vựng
  • rank first / account for / the highest / the lowest / considerably higher than / moderate level / in contrast

The charts compare the average weekly working hours of eight professions and the proportion of stress-related illness among people in these occupations.

Overall, business men and movie producers worked the longest hours, while lecturers and chefs had the shortest working weeks. In terms of stress, lawyers accounted for the largest share, whereas programmers experienced the lowest levels.

Business men worked the most, averaging 70 hours per week, followed by movie producers at 60 hours. Doctors also had relatively long working weeks at 52 hours, while writers worked 45 hours and programmers 40. Lawyers averaged 35 hours, and the figures were lower for chefs and lecturers, at 30 and 25 hours respectively.

Regarding stress-related illness, lawyers represented the highest proportion at 25%, despite having shorter working hours than several other professions. Movie producers followed at 18%, while doctors accounted for 15%. Business men made up 11% of stress cases, and chefs 10%. Writers and lawyers each contributed 8%, whereas programmers had the lowest figure at 5%.
